Thanks again for following us along, and feel free to share your thoughts with us here.<|endoftext_R9VQqF0Ag7|>Get ready for anticipation about the next major experiment in uniform design in the National Hockey League. The league unveiled the official logo for the alternate jerseys Tuesday morning at a press conference. The new alternate jerseys bearing the Florida Panthers and Colorado Avalanche logos will make their debut later this season. "We’ve been working on this for the last couple years," NHL commissioner Colin Campbell confirmed to NHL.com Tuesday. "We started the process early last year, which certainly created challenges for our design team. We had to make a couple unconventional decisions, but we’re pretty close and we’re on time." Chicago’s Barstool was the first website to share the image, which appears to be a mock-up of the alternate jersey. .@NHL confirms this is the new alternate jersey design bearing the 2017 Tree of Life logo 🦀🧡🧄‍♀️⚠📈 pic.twitter.com/pDmYcvOsRZ — Barstool (@barstool) July 23, 2016 [NHL's new alternate jersey designs: All-new jerseys, emblems] The jerseys haven’t changed much. More outdoor-themed logos can be seen on the new alternate jersey design, starting with the sleeves of the jersey for outdoor games, the shoulder pads for outdoor day games where fans can see the performance and pieces of the history of the Panthers and Avalanche. The band of the jerseys doesn’t change much either. If anything it shows a different version of the Tree of Life on top of the jersey, outlined in green. So how do you keep the secret of what this year’s design was going to be? "We were given that freedom and there were a couple of things that we thought we would do," NHL lead alternate designer Jim Caplan said during the league’s press conference. "The league wanted us to redo the team logo on the front of the jersey. We went the big route and kept the home and away logo and put the jersey and the sleeves on there with the tree of life because we got a ton of requests. "That was something very exciting for the fans to see that they could select the Tampa Bay Lightning but they had to put an alternate logo on there so they could make their choice. Caplan said the shape of the jersey was designed to reflect light for outdoor games and look more solid for outdoor games – a true goal jersey – which the league saw as a compromise. They chose to use white shells for outdoors, a plain white shell for outdoor and a neon-colored shell for outdoors. The fake sunskins were actually a sign of reflecting the sun at outdoor games and not a new NHL idea. However, it was one of the biggest talking points when designing the final result of the alternate jersey. NHL officials said one of the biggest challenges for the 2016-17 season is to get the players, coaches, executives, owners and teams to embrace and be strong supporters of alternate-style jerseys. NHL officials made a decision based on fan feedback not to wear home and away jerseys but for jerseys with black, white and neon primary colors. Campbell said the league wanted to take the risk and work with the fans to embrace alternate jerseys. "One of the most significant things we had to manage as we went along is listening," Campbell said about avoiding confusion. "It’s significant because we’re changing our obsessions and we’re offering a choice to our fans who have been with us for the past 102 years." In addition to the logo on the sleeves and sleeves, new graphics were added to the shoulder pads and sweaters all relating to the uniforms and logos. A national panel of about 3,000 lucky folk competed to select the tearfoot wrapper for the Waste Management Organization. There were many polyester products submitted to the agency’s website, but Yunma’s tearfoot was the winner. Tearfoot is completely biodegradable and is corrosion-resistant. Dr. Marina Vartan, a physician practicing in Brazil, stated earlier this year that, after three and a half years usage, patients begin to trust the product and feel comfortable using it on their skin. “It’s become very popular with my patients,” Vartan says. A wrapper made of the world’s first woven fabric, Daima Tearfoot took about seven years of development. It turns out to be durable, lightweight, corrosion-resistant, beautiful, gentle on skin, and water absorbing. We’re happy to report that at least a single corporation has been able to conduct a final test—thus eliminating the need for in situ testing of any kind—and asked whether we could send test batches. This could soon become a more common occurrence. Built from nature, Daima Tearfoot is made from the wastewater from Tearfoot Island. The company uses a very small amount of rainwater as a activator in the filtration process. This increases the “eco-density” of wastewater and in the process increases its corrosion resistance—ensuring structural durability. The first high quality fabric product made from wastewater on a commercial scale, Daima Tearfoot is designed for the world’s most environmentally conscious consumers: the retail and manufacturing industry. (Mohamed Azakir/Reuters) Protestors gathered in the Lebanese capitol of Beirut on Saturday to protest conditions at the refugee camp and oppression by Israel before being met with violent action by the Lebanese Army. Extosing the sign “stop camp clearance,” Lebanese police officers from the embassy ordered the demonstrators to take down their banners and leave. The protesters refused, and expressed disappointment and anger before being sent fleeing in a white van by the Lebanese Army. The demonstrators carried the Palestinian flag and sang a national anthem. The marchers chanted and shouted slogans in Arabic that included “genocide,” “sexual trafficking of children” and “women’s rights.” Demonstrators said they were calling for better living conditions in the camps of the city and for the relocation of Palestinian refugees from Israel. Refugees, the demonstrators said, compound the countries illnesses by often living on their soil while the government, which has recently launched efforts to annex Israeli settlements, fails to address the issue in return. The protest, which took place at 1:30 p.m. in the neighborhood of Aabed, is part of an ongoing struggle to reclaim land in Lebanon. The goal, the protestors explained, is to ensure that the Lebanese land is used for the good of their people and not another Israeli colony. Lebanon hosts two U.N. refugee camps that they have historically deemed very dangerous. A pair of smuggling tunnels, at one point, were discovered between Gaza and the Golan Heights. Demonstrators protesting the conditions of the camps said they aimed to counter Lebanon’s silence when it comes to the plight of Palestinian refugees. Lebanese police recently said they agreed with the Lebanese government that sending Palestinian refugees to be settled in Israel is a “bad idea” and “a zero-sum game,” while the Lebanese religious authority, al-Azhar, said that refugees sent from Israel should be allowed to stay. The United Nations agency for refugees, UNHCR, called the situation unacceptable in a Nov. 25 press release. As a result, Galegini’s theory received some attention from social science for creating the reality of authenticity. More recently, Eaton, Bogaold, and Gilman (2012) reviewing research on authenticity, have painted a similar picture.[3] The self-referential nature of Galegini’s communitarian method is thus a nice check on what is often a self-conscious social interaction. However, it begs the question whether Galegini’s method is actually useful, especially given the important ways that Galegini’s theory affects sociological method. This question is a difficult one. In this note, we wish to examine the self-referentiality of communiological method and bring it into the context of group reflection. Galegini’s self-conscious method aims at making people consciously aware of what is happening. Appealing immediately to submission as an example, this means that all group members should be aware of the submission process. If this is happening, then it should feel justified. Every single member of the group needs to have information about the details of the submission process, i.e., the specific criteria. Moreover, every single member should also be aware of the specific criteria themselves, which is also very useful for establishing social norms (although a skeptical cynic will argue that the group members themselves do not know about the criteria and so will not follow the norm either – that is a different question). If everyone is not informed about the criteria, the different backgrounds, professions, mannerisms etc. are a confusing constraint in the process. But if one person emerges as the “watchboy” for the criteria, then others will feel obliged to follow the criteria. The leader will also have a status, which can set expectations as to the group’s correct performance and increase confidence in the submission process. This will (hopefully) reduce pressure for correct norms. If this seems too far away from the everyday gestures of filling in pens on the paper, Galegini’s claim rings very true, because Saeed and Deane (2009) found that having different people perform more personally acceptable cues is very effective at increasing recognition. A related question within modern constructivity is the observation that we often do not know (or, more accurately, cannot explain) exactly what is happening at work. Most of us know what is in front of us and what we need to work on, but we are not aware of the reasons for certain actions. This explains why it often seems impossible to follow norms in the workplace. Galegini argues that everyone needs to be consciously aware of what is happening and so he aims to facilitate this appreciation by presenting the process in a friendly way. His method calls for everyone’s involvement, but doesn’t demand the willingness to get involved in the details of what they are doing. We would therefore argue that unless everyone admits it work, they may lose the motivation to consistently follow the right criteria (as opposed to Žižek’s lazy, “let’s make this stupid” spirit that his method tries to replace.[2]) Naturally, the most striking example of the self-referentiality of Galecki’s theory and related methods is found within the context of book review. One of the most criticised aspects of book reviews, at least in the lay literature, is their granularity. It feels like each book (and each project) has its own specific “rule”. This can be confusing for the assignment-sufferer – it feels as if every review assignment contains a new book/project/set of rules. The granularity of book reviews has been largely challenged by the introduction of multiple review criteria. Of course this super-priimal approach is not new for the context of critique (or for other contexts) but readers can now easily make sense of what is happening within the submission process. Here, we can again observe the self-referentiality of Galegini’s theory, which makes his method tailor-made for the context of critique. The multiple review criteria for a critique assignment essentially emphasises the acceptance of the critique process. It is reassuring, because the group is engaged in what they are doing. The sequence of requirements might have been replaced by a single requirement, but it is otherwise still there. If there is an uphill road to dry land, then you are still trying to get to dry land, with every single step, without noticing that the road is uphill. In a similar way, the whole group faces an uphill road to success. Since you are all aiming for success together, the multiple review criteria encourage others to see the exception to the rule. They conducted a series of experiments that started when the eggs were still on their way to the laboratory and were conducted over a number of weeks. The eggs were also temporarily removed, only starting at suspension, before the head and shell were separated by large, sealed plates. This effectively controlled their shape and after 63 days the genetic fragments were finally restored to their original form. Coried within the shell was a brand new duplicate clone in the nucleus. This process is known as "deploidification". "These results provide unequivocal laboratory evidence indicating that epigenetic deploidification is initiated and maintained in the course of the rabbit's development," the researchers explain in a scientific paper about the work. "These data indicate that deploidification might be viewed as a fundamental epigenetic mechanism occurring in primates raised on a clonal background and which may serve as a natural model for future studies." Despite the work, the researchers admit that the exact effects of deploidification remain unclear. Furthermore, further study may be necessary before more complex deploidification is achieved in a broad context. "I think my ankle is good, and I'm probably going to be able to go out and run on the floor pretty good. But I know as a whole, I'm going to work on my mechanics again. A CT scan came back from his surgery and physical testing is slated for Monday to see if he's cleared to play. The longer his rehab goes, the longer Walker's learning curve gets. will leave no stone unturned to make enhanced transparency a legal reality, and to help an evolving food industry that is going to need as much transparency as it can get. The Big Coin: A Solution For Human Driven Supply Chain Management Human and technology need to work together to create a more trustworthy food ecosystem. A trustworthy digital currency is a game-changer that would make a dramatic difference to supply chain management across many sectors. Although many sectors of the food industry strive to implement basic safety measures for safeguarding supply chain processes (such as auditing or verification of duplicate documentation or processing), they are facing significant challenges in addressing supply chain friction points. Predators can circumvent trace-testing methods, reproducible risk models are complex and costly, and the shelf life of branded goods can expire overnight. The EU and the FAO hosted SDG8 symposiums to address these challenges; progress is being made by a variety of collaborative initiatives. In spite of these efforts, rogue supply chain friction points are getting worse — the recent reports of contaminate gelatin in packaged fresh produce, contaminated soy milk in grape juice, and fecal-sourced bacteria found in beef products are notable examples. Recent public efforts by industry consortiums to push improvements in packaging, such as plastic kitchen utensils and reusable straws convey the impression that the bad old days of lax food safety are over. But these measures do not address the underlying obstacle to supply chain operations. Every year billions of dollars move through ‘chain’ food entities — ‘chain’ is an intentionally misleading term — often in schemes that are vulnerable to misuse and fail to promote human dignity. Often routine trade transfers must be conducted in cash, because a bank is an intermediary. Nothing can be done to cut down on transfer fees, delays, or other unintended factors that can lead to added reasons for fraud. There is no universal framework to prevent abuse of prepaid-time provisions, the resaleing of packaged goods with an extended expiration date, or obscuring the supply chain from poor corporate management and procurement decisions. A trustworthy digital currency, such as Bigcoin, which is universally accepted, fast, and inherently transactional, could change all of this. A transactional digital currency forces human and technology to work together. It creates a strong incentive to refrain from commercial behavior that could easily lead to compromised food safety, including procurement practices that could lead to fake sourcing, misreportsing questionable quality, and counterfeiting goods through prepaid-time clauses. Additionally, Bigcoin can be used in cash payments or non-cash related ways such as tipping, which introduces human dignity into the supply chain without encouraging responsible behavior. A trustworthy digital currency builds on bitcoin’s growing popularity and popularity, and on its unique open source strength as a distributed financial instrument. The technology exists to take on real currency among supply chains and financial institutions as a way to promote integrity. These entities do not have to directly adopt such a currency. Rather than asking merchants to directly pay intermediaries, which would be impossible for many businesses to implement, Bigcoin could instead be accepted through a variety of “crypto payments.” This would significantly increase opportunities to detect fraud, promote transparency, and promote human dignity in the food supply chain through an easily accessible and tradeable digital currency. Trusted currencies can build public confidence in the integrity of food entities and their supply chains. A troubling trust gap between food system stakeholders and public participation is widening, and we’re seeing it everywhere. While the public is increasingly vocal about attempts to limit access to data, many agencies remain under pressure from private industry. Even Scott Pruitt’s leadership of the EPA, who announced that he is pushing back the distribution of Roundup Ready herbicide power indefinitely yet again, remains powerless, in the words of the New York Times’ Michael Barron, to reestablish the relationship between private business and government. But even successful re-establishment isn’t good enough, because public trust has become severely strained. In addition to new chemical developments, advances in artificial intelligence present a new challenge to society. Automatic behavior changes, in the form of AI, are seeping into every corner of our lives and leading to an alarming lack of transparency around AI developments in our food system. Technology is only starting to deliver evidence-based, ethically-motivated transparency in the food system. Simply substituting traditional stickers and coins is not the answer. There is urgent need for dynamic, open source, open data and innovative digital signature solutions that can restore both transparency and public trust. Political action is needed to codify efforts like Bigcoin, the Blockchain Protocol, and trustworthy digital currencies. A trustworthy currency would be a major catalyst for transparency and human dignity in an increasingly digitized food, food logistics, and economic sectors du jour. It could radically invigorate food supply chain processes by providing incentives that reinforce responsible behavior. This is no small feat, and in the current environment

But first, let’s take a look at some of the ways you can save money from various spending scenarios. How does fiat currency come into being? The short answer is that money, aka the “quantity of money,” is the mechanism that determines the amount of money produced in an economy. The “money supply” is always limited in quantity because there is a government-granted mechanism that generates new money by allowing banks to print checks to pay for loans to businesses. Additionally, the inflow of new money is contained to a limited degree as businesses refuse to spend old money and banks lend only on new debt. Yet, money in circulation doesn’t change over time, so the demand for money should remain constant. For proof, consider the graphic below: Ultimately, there are only three primary holders of money in an economy: businesses, banks, and consumer money. Because of this structure, the money supply is governed by the natural annual rate of increase in the primary money. Thus, the general interest rate reflects the natural rate of increase in the national quantity of money. For example, Federal Reserve data (10-year averages) show that in the first three months of a year, the interest rate is 10%. During the remainder of the year, the rate is 3%. In the first quarter of 2015, the rate is 0.25%. It’s important to note that the interest rate is strictly supply-adjusted, meaning that the more variable the quantity of money, the greater the demand for money is because demand is elastic compared to the quantity in circulation. Even the highest-quality gold bar is still backed by a smaller amount of gold than nearly one in circulation, and so demand has to be high. The supply calculation also means that as long as the amount of money in circulation is equal to the amount of fiat currency in circulation, an interest rate remains deflated. In other words, even though the quantity of money is currently limited, there will always be a growing demand for money. Let’s consider consumption demand, for example in the store or at a restaurant. Obviously, the higher the price for purchase, the greater demand for money. But supply needed to meet prices is not always rising. So, why doesn’t consumption demand grow with inflation? It’s actually for a couple of economic reasons: first, because money is scarce, and second, currency fluctuation, which applies to both spending and investment. The first factor—currency fluctuation—affects the need to inflate the currency. Inflation reduces the spending factor because higher prices require more fiat currency in circulation to meet demand. It’s also true that whenever a new currency enters circulation, the demand will be higher and vice versa. In essence, this provides a strong economic incentive for extravagant spending, which can continue so long as the quantity of money in circulation remains fixed. Interestingly, while currency fluctuation is relevant to spending from one state to the next, it is also relevant to investment because the currency is typically variable across states, varies from state to state, and rates to supply. need to take action. There was a report last week which said the trend of sea level rise is slowing. Interesting news? It is not recognising the urgency of the situation. For instance, even if it is slowing, we know that many more glaciers are still calving, and that water is moving from oceans towards land. That's going to be the biggest climate impact. We need to start doing that work now, otherwise it will be postponed. Until we are absolutely prepared, we are really screwed. A process of adaptation is taking place. But we have to have a sense where, instead of trying to run from it, we have to work with it. We really need to start doing that. Because, at the moment, we are building right on top of it. We have to start building in a different way. With a lot of the infrastructure we are building in the Netherlands, we are causing sea levels to go up, or glaciers or volcanoes. It is only recently that climate change has become a bit more real for me, and other people in my generation, and I think it has tightened up some in the last year or so. I am all for climate action. Climate change isn't automatically put in place, so it takes a political commitment and a framework provided by the government to help us see all the things come together and what critical space we are actually in. It's not that we have to cut down on taxes or public spending. We've got to really try to cover all the sectors. We have a different investment system here. We don't care about building any new coal power plants, because we have too many gas-fired plants already in place. Instead, the government set up a Green Investment Fund, where we make long-term investments into renewable energy. The Green Investment Fund model is already being used in the Netherlands, and we want to develop it. It is worth adopting and building on because it helps us to measure how we want to tackle climate change. There is one constant: that pricing clean energy is not going to fluctuate too much in the future, that it is no longer consistently cheaper than fossil fuel. There are also questions of making sure the grid is designed to allow us to capitalise on solar energy, and on wind turbines. Those are the choices we make. There is no other country where we can do the same thing. It's not just a financial cost, it's about the impact. When we have a World Cup in the Netherlands, it means every bit as much to a country somewhere in the world as it does to us. We can take that attention and use it to create good-quality jobs - which we do not here. The regulatory proposal discussed at the start of this post refers to the administration’s proposal to eliminate the gasoline rebate caps on buyers of new cars and tractors. The action will make it more costly to implement the regulatory rollbacks on new cars and trucks and thus encourage worse consumer choices. For example, eliminating the gasoline rebate cap will encourage consumers to use a new $7 billion federal tax credit to purchase low-energy and hybrid and other vehicles. Granted, the new tax credit will generate enough revenue to offset the decline in prices over the course of the five-year rule. However, in that time prices paid by many buyers will go up. The introduction of a 25 percent federal excise tax on new cars and trucks in response to the regulatory change will also discourage many consumers from purchasing new cars and trucks for reasons other than avoidance of federal taxes. These choices will also produce more of the less efficient vehicles, such as minivans, that don’t offer tax credits and are thus harder to sell. Protecting the interests of savvy consumers surely represents a major priority for the Trump administration, given the major benefits resulting from statutory restrictions on government and regulatory benefits. 2. As a result of this fact, economists who favor consumer benefits from lifting the fuel rebate spending cap may not get a good estimate of how much will go to consumers. Consumer benefits from fuel economy improvements are always contingent on auto producers’ adoption of efficiency trends which can vary wildly from year to year as a result of factors unrelated to cost. Health care professionals and senior residents in group homes worry that sending vulnerable residents outdoors would increase the risk of their contracting the virus. "Operationally, it doesn't make sense," said Sylvia Abramichich, a group home resident and native of Rhode Island, who also lives at home with her parents. "You have everything from the elderly to her toddler in the bedroom, a bathroom and a shower. It just doesn't make sense." But concerns about privacy-a concept that all Rhode Island residents hold to be sacred-and older residents' daily needs cannot be ignored, especially at this unprecedented period in human history. The Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC) recommends minimizing close contact with others, unless under the supervision of a healthcare professional and practicing social distancing when possible. CDC also lists activities that large groups of people should avoid doing to avoid spreading the virus, including gathering in the same place, washing their hands quickly, avoiding off-the-counter medications and washing their hands before use. The Group Home Association, the national trade association for group homes, says that imposing limits on visitors reflects the balance between maintaining safe communities while protecting vulnerable residents. It also does not support an outright ban on external visits at this time. "In order to minimize the spread of the disease in group homes, we support the CDC guidance recommending the avoidance of groups of more than 10 people," GHA spokesman Scott Phillips said. "We are not advocating a total ban on visits. The point is that residential centers should be screening visitors and requiring their residents to stay indoors, just as health care facilities have used [patient screening] for a long time." The American Academy of Pediatrics (AAP) has warned school administrators against sending children and teenagers outside on their own for prolonged periods of time. And public health officials are advising older people to stay at home except if they need medical care. Both the Rhode Island AHA and the Federal National Institute for Occupational Safety and Health (NIOSH) have issued regulations urging Americans to practice good hygiene and engage in social distancing and reduce physical gatherings to curb the spread of the deadly virus. The beds should be under six feet in size, or approximately 3.5 feet by 9.5 feet with 5.5 feet of distance drywall between the beds, with a hallway in the middle of each bed. It also advises owners that their group homes must feature private dining areas, play rooms that can accommodate children and small couches where residents can lie together. Beds should be placed as far as possible from open spaces, and residents should only be allowed to open their room to a visitor when asked. NIOSH state policy director Jeryl King emphasized that group homes should not be confused with nursing homes. Thus, the Toolkit is quite accurate at predicting homicides, with the exception of causing a (modest) rise in the total number of nonfatal shootings, which was precisely my initial intent from the top of the article. The accuracy of the model isn’t limited to overall, population-adjusted output, however: it is extended to a regional, population-adjusted basis. First, the model is sensitive to local and regional differences. This means that when the model corrects for homicide rates in states by standard deviation to the median, it replaces the median number of officers with a median incarcerated population. This, we have already seen, is because a deterioration in the number of officers has been occurring for some time, so local differences are less important than in baseline the number of officers. Second, the model is sensitive of big-picture changes, such as the effects of demographics and changes in the overall population. This means that when the model corrects for the number of law enforcement officers by standard deviation, it typically produces a more pronounced decline than any past trends in the number of officers, which in turn produces larger standard deviations on a population-adjusted basis. Since the model is highly sensitive to demographics, much of the recent decline in the number of officers is due to demographic changes, which have encouraged many officers to leave the force. It also becomes clear from the model that rising resignation rates among officers of retirement age could be responsible for at least some part of the drop in overall homicide rates in recent years. This is because, while the number of active officers is always included as part of the overall output, officers of retirement age rejoin much earlier than the general population, so they have more opportunities to leave. Thus, the large declines we see in the number of active officers in recent years are in large part due to higher retention rates among older officers. The Government has opposed the "all advertising is the same" requirement for preventing the use of effective messaging in warnings, saying the trial should continue and that all advertisements would be continually reassessed. 7.30 contacted the South Australian Department of Health about the decision and the trial. DOH response does not refer to a ban on signs advertising e-cigarettes In its response, the Department omitted references to a ban on signs advertising e-cigarettes. "The Department has reassessed all advertising including advertising e-cigarettes, continually reassessing the effectiveness of all labels on the latest versions, in connection with ongoing trial reviews," the statement said. "Where the trial on e-cig advertising is closed or in written review, the Department has received notification on a trial review." The questions went to the Department of Environment and Planning of the Department of Health and the National Tobacco Control Inspectorate. A spokesman from the Department directed the correspondence to the Department. 7.30 has obtained copies of the replies. Move could be interpreted as 'blind partisanship', expert says State Opposition health spokesman James Warburton questioned the need for the trial. "It's ironic, after being forced to accept cigarette smoke was no worse than cigarettes, it's no worse than e-cigarettes," he said. He said the policy appeared as a one-sided approach, which risked promoting ineffective advertising and eroding the credibility of public health. "There are sometimes cases where the government says 'no, there's no risk', but this is a one-sided situation where we don't have a trial, it's implemented based on what public health authorities are prepared to do, what the government is prepared to tell people to do." CSWA senior policy fellow Judith Wright said the Department's decision was driven by personal ideological policy rather than science. "This is a situation in which political ideology, vested interests and I would add political influence have a huge impact on the making of public policy," she said. "It's not just a bad decision — this is not the right process for the making of public policy." Wright said it was not normal for the Government to oppose issuing health warnings about new products before they are available. "It's blind partisanship that we should not allow in the policy realm," she said. "This gives rise to the idea that whenever there is new innovation in the market it is subject to censorship, and that is a very unfortunate situation." Effective advertising Reakord researcher Joseph Craven said banning e-cigarettes advertising would have a detrimental effect on the harm reduction of tobacco use. "While we've got no clear evidence on advertising harm reduction, we have quite strong evidence on the effectiveness of the messages in encouraging smokers to reduce harm by quitting," he said. "In fact, advertising effectiveness studies suggest that much of the harm reduction advertising reduces the effectiveness of the warning." Dr Craven pointed to a Swedish study which found advertising from cigarette brands dropped by 26 per cent following a "no advertising" advertising policy. Craven said the minimising and removal of the health benefits of e-cigarettes would lead to a massive rise in smoking prevalence as a result of the ban. [2] Electricity production analyses have revealed the different energy expenditures that are required to produce commodities depending on the scale different crops are grown at and the use of various agricultural inputs. The diagram below, using the area of a corn and soybean field, illustrates the amount of electricity each common crop input would need to directly produce. [3] Thus electricity consumption analyses can be used to estimate not just production costs, but the crop and agricultural energy expenditures required to produce commodities, a metric that can enable us to measure the efficiency and sustainability of our production activities across the agricultural sector. It will take into account expenditures that are currently allowed to take place in the industry and the energy input they represent. Assumptions of energy Most crops we produce need to have completed their life cycle long before getting to market. This process is largely dependent on the quality of the soil, vegetation, and local conditions. Each commodity can go through unique growth stages that require a unique agricultural input. When we talk about energy, one of the most commonly used reference metrics is Mileage Eqution Efficiency (FFE), which measures how much energy is required to drive a truck from the airport to the drive-thru. As the name implies, we can apply this metric to the yield of a product, measured by the total energy needed to produce it per unit of labor (man-hours) and materials (land, water, etc.) used. Diagram of a pre-production system. Image from farmoftheplanet.com. While generally MTEs are very similar between products made in the Pacific Northwest and those made in Georgia or West Virginia, they are very different between products made in California or New York and those made in Mexico. Each commodity, however, has unique growth stages that require a unique agricultural input, which vary based on the characteristics of the soil and vegetation. These differences can be measured by combining the agricultural electricity input to produce commodities with the metabolic value. Thus, the calculation takes into account electricity expenditures at harvest, levels of crop productivity and differences in levels of moisture to facilitate growing a given crop variety. The calories used in the commodity are then divided by the crop's metabolic value, a quantity that reflects the average energy consumed in producing agricultural products. To determine a metabolic value, long field-trails take place. Single crops alone are not enough to calculate energy expenditures at harvest. If we replace a field of alfalfa, maize, cotton or corn with neighboring fields of corn, wheat, soybean or wheat then the level of electricity expenditures will increase. While we can assume that the magnitude of this increase will be proportional to the biomass yield, productivity and nutrient composition of the crops in that area, the magnitude of this increase in the energy consumption at harvest will be largely dependent on the vitamin and protein contents of the alternative crops. For example, if every field in the area produces manure mixed with hematite, nitrates, or other ingredients, the new crop's high level of protein production could result in a large increase in the crop energy consumption at harvest, but not if all the fields in the area are relatively simple crops. So how important is energy consumption at harvest when calculating production costs? A systematic review of agricultural subsidies, published in 2004, that used data from many different sources showed that crop energy consumption at harvest is highly correlated with levels of crop productivity. While there was not enough evidence to prove a direct correlation from the electricity expenditures required to directly cultivate a field to the level of crop productivity, the authors found that crop energy consumption at harvest was on average more than 5 times as high as crop productivity was, in the range of 8 times the crop biomass energy required to produce each pound produced in a field. Furthermore, over multiple decades, the difference between crop and crop energy consumption at harvest increased (by one order of magnitude). Materials used to estimate agricultural energy expenditures at harvest in a systematic manner are limited, as they do not have a good representation for the large diversity of activities on a farm. There is, however, room for improvement. For example, one research team has explored building a spectrometer that can estimate energy expenditures. They managed to demonstrate that a 1% increase in the energy input required to move cattle from pasture to feed or slaughter facility on a typical American farm was enough to cause a 2% increase in the production costs. Due to the increase in energy used to move cattle to pasture, there was as much additional electricity from diesel generators to transport cattle to the barn to fill the new milking facilities for the pasture destructulation of the cattle industry, as it cost in electricity to move cows from the farm to slaughter. [4] Electricity expenditures at harvest probably are not as important compared to the expenditures that they could be replacing, such as when supplying electricity to oil and gas fields, or timber or ethane mines.
